text: So we will discuss the laparoscopic splenectomy. Spleen is hidden within the rib cage unless until it is double in the size. So normal spleen cannot be palpable. It is within the costal margin. Your target is hilum. This is your target. And according to that you have to draw two arcs. One is another is twenty-four. So, telescope should be ten centimeter lateral in twenty-four. Two umbilicus, one port will be here and one port will be here.

Spleen has five important surrounding ligaments. That is pancreatosplenic ligament. This is a ligament. Gastroesplenic ligament. This is a gastroesplenic ligament which we can see. And this is phrenicoesplenic ligament. These are main supportive ligament. And inferiorly you have two ligament. One is phrenicocolic ligament and another is esplenocolic ligament. This is splenocolic ligament and this is frenicocolic ligament. Means from diaphragm to colon and spleen to colon. There are different approach of doing splenectomy like anterior approach, lateral approach, inferior approach. French people they do lateral approach. Americans generally they do anterior approach and inferior approach. Indication, case selection is important. Although you can do any explain, but most of the autoimmune diseases are better to deal with the laparoscopy. Like ITP, autoimmune hemolytic anemia, microspherocytosis, benign tumor and cyst and HIV related thrombocytopenia. These are the best indication.

Hematological malignancy and moderate splenomegaly is a contraindication. But relative contraindication. And massive splenomegaly and portal hypertension is an absolute contraindication. Because in massive splenomegaly you may not have that much space to put your trocar. like if the patient has Kalajar or others, in this massive esophagus, sometimes it is up to the umbilicus. You cannot do laparoscopy. Patient should be semi-lateral position. Left side should be up and semi-lateral position. Hands should be supported. Sajan will stand on the ventral aspect, one assistant on the dorsal aspect, and the scrub nurse will be here. Monitor should be at this point. near the shoulder and if you want two monitor one monitor for the assistant this way you will put the your monitor present is semi lateral position this is the position of the present the arm is supported and this is the costal margin this is the iliac crest here is the iliac crest And this is the way how the patient is draped.

The surgeon positions the port ten centimeters lateral to the umbilicus, near the mid-clavicular line, adjusting based on spleen size. The varus needle is inserted perpendicularly for access, followed by irrigation, suction, and insufflation tests. A disposable trocar is introduced, and the telescope is inserted to visualize the splenic hilum and establish the arc for surgical navigation.

The surgeon demonstrates the port positions for laparoscopic splenectomy, highlighting the costal margin and hilum. Two ports are positioned contralaterally to create a sixty-degree angle, forming a diamond shape. An additional port can be placed 7.5 centimeters laterally for a liver retractor, ensuring all ports align within the same arc. The telescope is positioned 24 centimeters away for optimal mobilization and panoramic view.

The surgeon positions the ports for laparoscopic splenectomy, with the telescope placed ten centimeters lateral to the umbilicus. The inferior pole of the spleen is mobilized by freeing the splenocolic and phrenicocolic ligaments, allowing the spleen to be retracted upwards for better visibility of the hilum. The short gastric vessels are dissected using a stapler or ligature harmonic, followed by dissection and clipping or stapling of the hilum to separate it.

The surgeon uses a hook to separate the inferior pole of the spleen from the splenocolic and phrenocolic ligaments, lifting the lower pole upwards. This is the initial step in the splenectomy procedure, preparing for the dissection of short gastric vessels and subsequent hilum dissection.

Only one percent is doing by the clip or suture. If you want to be your life easy, then you should do by the stapler. If someone is using the stapler for appendicectomy, I am not in the favor. You should do by the knot. But for splenectomy, use of a stapler is better. Because if you want to do with the knot or with the clip, you have to do a lot of dissection. You need to make a window between the artery and vein. You have to do the perihilar entire fat to nicely dissect. But with the stapler, a lot of your energy will be saved. Directly you can fire and you can do it much easier and much short time. White color cartridge has to be used. twelve millimeter port is required. So you have to enlarge the incision to make twelve millimeter and then port will be introduced. This is endo-GI linear stapler. You can use tri-stapler. You can use Aslon-sixty also by the Corvidian. And you will use white color cartridge as you know that In this stapler, if you will pull this back, the jaw opens. And if you close this one, jaw closes. Before firing, you have to press this button. After that, you can fire. So, different color coding is B for bubble, G for green in the muscle, and W, white for vessels. White can be used as a universal. Some people, they use for bubble also, the white. because white doesn't have a predefined bending point. You know it is very important to learn that every stapler has predefined bending point. Like suppose this is a stapler, then they have a, this is a bending point. Company is hammering it here. And why it is hammered? Because when you press it, it bends from the bending point. Am I clear? It will bend. You will take a rod and you hammer the rod at one place and then bend it, it will bend from where? Where it is hammered. This bending point. But in the vascular staplers, the bending point is here. at this corner because if they will define the bending point it will bleed. Let us take one example if your stapler is made for fifteen staples and if you are stapling fifty then it will make a C. You are stapling fifty pages then it will make a shape of a C. And if it is made for fifty pages and you are stapling only two pages, then what will happen? It will be loose. It will be loose, there will be gap between the papers. And if it is a predefined staple line, vessels with the blood look eight mm. But after excusing the blood out, it looks two mm or three mm. So how can you decide the thickness of the vessel? So that's why white cartridge, when you fire the staple, It bends like that. It doesn't make a shape of a bee. Because making the shape of a bee is risky. If it is thick, it will make a shape of a C. And if it is thin, then this bee will be loose. And from that much loose space, bleeding will happen. That much loose space, blood will flow. So that's why white color is universal.

The surgeon uses a white cartridge stapler to secure and cut the splenic hilum, applying three rows of staples on either side with a cut in between. The hilum is held securely in the stapler before firing. After firing, the staple line is visible, and any minor oozing is addressed with titanium clips. The procedure progresses to detaching remaining fibers of the phrenicosplenic ligament using a hook, completing the splenectomy.

The surgeon loads the cartridge into the stapler, inserts it through a 12mm cannula, and opens the jaw using a left-hand grasper to position the pedicle between the jaws. After ensuring the pedicle is evenly distributed, the surgeon fires the stapler, advancing the blade 15mm before retracting it to open the jaws again. The spleen is then placed into a size D endo bag, which features a metal ring and purse string for secure closure, allowing for the organ's removal.

The surgeon uses a metal ring endo bag to extract the spleen. The endo bag is inserted through a 12mm port, and the piston mechanism ejects the metal ring inside the abdominal cavity. The metal ring scoops the spleen into the bag, and pulling the purse string detaches the ring from the plastic, closing the bag's mouth. The spleen is then morselated using ovum forceps within the endo bag before extraction. The wound is closed using skin stapler, glue, or sutures.

The surgeon closes the port site at the end of the splenectomy procedure, injecting xylocaine to prevent post-operative pain. An ester strip is applied to the incision. The video transitions to a partial splenectomy for a patient with a splenic abscess. The surgeon uses a stapler with purple cartridges, which functions as a grasper and then fires staples, cutting tissue with three rows of staples on either side. The stapler has variable heights for firing and includes a sharp blade for cutting.

The surgeon uses a tri-stapler with variable height staples to secure and cut tissue during the splenectomy. The cartridge jaw and face jaw work together, with the sharp blade cutting between the three rows of staples on either side. Gore-Tex tape is applied to prevent bleeding when stapling delicate spleen tissue.

The surgeon demonstrates the use of a tri-stapler for partial splenectomy. The cartridge is separable, with a blue color indicating its use. Gore-Tex tape is applied on either side of the jaws to prevent bleeding during firing. The stapler can articulate up to sixty degrees, allowing for precise positioning. The cartridge is attached by aligning arrows on the gun and cartridge, then rotating to lock it in place. The surgeon shows how to open and close the jaws and articulates the stapler before firing.

The surgeon applies Gore-Tex tape to the tri-stapler and fires it to perform a partial splenectomy, ensuring staple compression prevents bleeding. The cut spleen is placed into a size D endo bag for removal. Suction irrigation is used to manage oozing, and a hemostatic matrix like Flowseal is applied to stop bleeding by initiating coagulation.

The surgeon uses a tripolar instrument to perform a splenectomy, coagulating and cutting short gastric vessels with the inbuilt blade. The tripolar is attached to a force triad high-frequency machine, enabling efficient coagulation. The surgeon carefully separates the splenocolic and phrenicocolic ligaments from the spleen, using traction to form a clear dissection plane. The tripolar functions as both a blunt and sharp dissector, while a Maryland retractor holds the sling away to prevent accidental piercing.

The surgeon carefully skeletonizes the splenic artery and vein using energy sources, ensuring complete exposure of the hilum. The tail of the pancreas is identified near the surgical field, requiring caution during dissection. The greater curvature of the stomach and short gastric vessels are visible, with the hilum fat being meticulously dissected.

The surgeon separates the fibers of the lower pole of the spleen from the colon, lifting the spleen to free it completely. There is a minor burn on the peritoneum of the abdominal wall due to overshooting with energy devices, but it is safe as it avoids bowel perforation. The pancreas is visible and requires careful handling. The lower pole is now free, and the hilum is retracted upwards, exposing the artery posteriorly and the vein anteriorly for skeletonization.

The surgeon removes peri-hilar fat to create a window between the splenic artery and vein. The lower pole of the spleen is lifted and mobilized. Using bipolar harmonic, the surgeon coagulates at two places and cuts between them to separate the artery and vein. The ligature technique is employed to seal and cut simultaneously, ensuring effective sealing.

The surgeon uses a blade to coagulate and cut the vein at two places, demonstrating the use of energy in splenectomy. The pulsating splenic artery is visible, and the surgeon successfully coagulates it with energy. The remaining short gastric vessels are addressed, and the video transitions to discussing the lateral approach for splenectomy.

The surgeon employs the lateral approach to mobilize the spleen dorsally, flipping it over the stomach. The spleen is repositioned from its original location to lie over the stomach, with the hilum visible and the left lobe of the liver adjacent to the spleen.

The surgeon demonstrates the lateral approach for splenectomy, showing the spleen flipped over the stomach with the hilum exposed. The splenic artery and vein are skeletonized and stapled separately using a 30 mm cartridge, preventing arteriovenous fistula formation. The spleen is mobilized, crossing the falciform ligament and nearing the gallbladder. The phrenico-splenic ligament is cut, and the spleen is freed from the short gastric vessels, ready for removal.

Complications are intraoperative bleeding, post-splectomy sepsis, wound and port side infection, pancreatic injury and most of the complications are technique related. Technique related means you are using suture or hemolog or clip or stapler. These all options are there. And you do the anterior approach or lateral approach. So this was regarding the esplanetron. Another advantage that suppose you will not be able to control that bleeding by laparoscopy, it will give you the best site to give incision. Isn't it? And you can convert to laparotomy. Patient is in the grade two or grade three shock, laparoscopy is contraindicated and airway should be correct.
